does: This paper introduces SafeVec, a white-box evaluation procedure that measures LLM safety by analyzing internal representations rather than generated outputs. The method extracts layer-wise refusal directions from a safety-aligned reference model, identifies stable layer windows where safe and unsafe behaviors are representationally separable, and scores target models by measuring whether their hidden states align with these refusal directions when given unsafe or jailbreak prompts. The resulting metric, RAS (Refusal Alignment Score), converts representation-level alignment measurements into a calibrated 0-100 safety score. The authors demonstrate that RAS effectively separates aligned models from uncensored and abliterated variants, correlates with output-level attack success rates, and addresses key limitations of traditional output-level evaluation including high computational cost, sensitivity to judge selection, and dependence on fixed question sets.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research examining the internal mechanisms of safety in language models. It extends prior work on mechanistic interpretability of alignment and safety features, which has established that aligned models develop distinct internal representations for safe versus unsafe behaviors. The paper draws on methods from representation learning and probing techniques that identify task-relevant directions in neural network activations. It also connects to research on jailbreak attacks and adversarial robustness in LLMs, using uncensored and abliterated model variants as test cases. The approach responds to limitations in existing safety evaluation frameworks, which rely on output-level judgment and have been critiqued for scalability and reliability issues. SafeVec combines insights from mechanistic interpretability with practical safety evaluation needs.
enables: This work opens several directions for future research. By demonstrating that safety can be evaluated through internal representations, it enables more efficient and scalable safety assessment that could reduce computational costs of evaluation. The refusal direction framework could support faster iteration on safety-aligned model development by providing immediate feedback on representation-level alignment. The stability analysis of refusal directions across layers may inform architectural design choices for more robustly aligned models. Additionally, this approach could enable transfer of safety properties across model families through systematic mapping of refusal directions, and support detection of sophisticated jailbreaks that might fool output-level evaluators. The calibrated RAS metric could also facilitate standardized safety benchmarking and support development of more targeted safety interventions based on which layer windows require strengthening.
